metadata.summary: Kanban boards in GitLab's DevOps platform with scoped labels (`::`-syntax) for state-machine semantics. Integrates with CI/CD, MR workflows, and premium iterations for single-platform agile delivery.

content.title: GitLab Boards
content.summary: GitLab Issue Boards provide kanban-style project management integrated directly with GitLab's DevOps platform. Boards visualize issues through customizable lists based on scoped labels, assignees, or milestones, enabling workflow management alongside CI/CD pipelines. Unifying source control, CI/CD, and project tracking in one platform eliminates context-switching overhead and enables automation that is impossible across separate tools — for example, auto-transitioning an issue to `workflow::review` when a linked MR is opened. Scoped labels give the board real state-machine semantics rather than tag soup.

why.p: Unifying source control, CI/CD, and project tracking in one platform eliminates context-switching overhead and enables automation that is impossible across separate tools — for example, auto-transitioning an issue to `workflow::review` when a linked MR is opened. Scoped labels give the board real state-machine semantics rather than tag soup.

list.item: Teams using GitLab for source control and CI/CD wanting a single-platform PM workflow.
Multi-project / multi-group programs where group-level boards aggregate cross-repo issues.
Compliance-conscious organizations that need self-hosted (Omnibus) over SaaS.
Workflows tied to MR lifecycle (issue → branch → MR → review → merge → close).
Teams running Iterations (sprints) with cadence automation built into GitLab.
Source not in GitLab — adopting boards-only via API is awkward; use a source-aligned tool (Jira, Linear, GitHub Projects).
Heavy portfolio + EVM needs — GitLab roadmaps are basic compared to Jira Plans or MS Project.
Non-technical stakeholders who refuse Markdown / quick-action syntax — adoption tax exceeds value.
Tiny teams that don't need WIP limits or scoped labels — Trello is faster with less ceremony.
Budget constrained to free tier but requiring WIP limits, multiple boards, or iterations (all Premium+).

section.name: board-setup
section.p: Board types, scoped label scheme, WIP limits, and board configuration rules.

rule.statement: Use project-level boards for single-repo workflows; use group-level boards for cross-project visibility in multi-team programs.
rule.rationale: Group boards aggregate issues across subgroups but inherit labels inconsistently — agents moving issues across groups can lose label context.

section.p: Board list types available: Label (workflow stages), Assignee (workload view), Milestone (release planning), Iteration (sprint board). Multiple boards per project requires GitLab Premium.

rule.statement: Use "::" syntax for all workflow, priority, and type labels to enforce mutual exclusivity — an issue can hold only one label per scope prefix.
rule.rationale: Without scoped labels, an issue can carry both "workflow::backlog" and "workflow::in-progress" simultaneously, corrupting board state.

example.title: Scoped label scheme
example.note: Scoped labels are mutually exclusive within their prefix. An issue transitions by swapping the label (e.g., workflow::backlog replaced by workflow::in-progress).

rule.statement: Keep total label count under 25 per project; archive or rename rather than accumulate.
rule.rationale: Label sprawl makes triage harder and slows label picker rendering in the UI.

rule.statement: Set WIP limits at the team's measured throughput, not aspirational capacity; tighten if cycle-time degrades.
rule.rationale: WIP limits backed by observed data are defensible; aspirational limits get ignored under pressure.

example.title: Board configuration with WIP limits
example.code: board_configuration: name: "Development Board" lists: - type: label label: "workflow::backlog" position: 0 - type: label label: "workflow::ready" position: 1 limit: 10 - type: label label: "workflow::in-progress" position: 2 limit: 5 - type: label label: "workflow::review" position: 3 limit: 3 - type: label label: "workflow::testing" position: 4 - type: closed position: 5 filters: milestone: "v1.0" labels: ["type::feature"]

rule.statement: Issue and MR templates must be version-controlled in ".gitlab/" and reviewed via MR like code, not edited through the UI.
rule.rationale: UI-only edits produce unreviewed changes to team process with no audit trail.

rule.statement: Use GitLab Iteration cadences with automatic creation for sprints; do not create iterations manually one by one.
rule.rationale: Cadence automation ensures consistent sprint length and reduces admin overhead.

example.title: Iteration cadence with automatic creation
example.code: iteration_cadence: title: "Development Sprints" start_date: "2024-01-01" duration: 2 # weeks upcoming: 4 automatic: true

section.name: automation
section.p: Rules for automating issue transitions via GitLab CI and REST API, including quick-action behavior and webhook routing.

rule.statement: Auto-transition issues via the REST API labels endpoint from CI jobs, not via quick-action syntax in description bodies created by PUT requests.
rule.rationale: Quick actions parse on POST (create/comment) but NOT on PUT (description edit) — explicit label API calls are reliable in all contexts.

example.title: CI-driven issue transitions via API
example.code: # .gitlab-ci.yml update_issue_on_mr: stage: notify script: - | curl --request PUT \ --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: $GITLAB_TOKEN" \ --data "labels=workflow::review" \ "$CI_API_V4_URL/projects/$CI_PROJECT_ID/issues/$ISSUE_ID" rules: - if: $CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID when: always

antipattern.description: Putting quick-action strings like "/label ~workflow::review" in a PUT request body to update issue description.
antipattern.reason: Quick actions only parse on initial POST creation or comment creation. PUTs to description do not re-trigger quick-action parsing, so the label is silently ignored.

rule.statement: Wire "Closes #N" into MR description as a CI lint rule; do not rely on humans to add it manually.
rule.rationale: Auto-close on merge requires the exact phrase in the MR description. Human compliance without enforcement is near zero on busy teams.

rule.statement: Use push options to set MR labels at branch creation time via "-o merge_request.label" instead of manually labeling post-creation.
rule.rationale: Agents and developers creating branches this way produce consistent label state without UI interaction.

example.title: Push options for MR creation with labels
example.code: git push -o merge_request.create \ -o merge_request.label="workflow::review" \ -o merge_request.assign="@reviewer" \ origin feature/my-branch

rule.statement: Subscribe webhooks at the project scope for narrow targeting; subscribe at system scope only when you need all projects and can filter on payload.
rule.rationale: Broad system webhooks deliver high volume; agents listening must filter carefully or process irrelevant events at cost.

rule.statement: Rotate webhook secret tokens carefully — breaking the signature check breaks all listening agents simultaneously without obvious error.
rule.rationale: GitLab webhook signature validation is token-based and does not provide a rotation grace period.

rule.statement: Triage Service Desk issues with labels only after content enrichment; never auto-close Service Desk issues without human review.
rule.rationale: Service Desk emails carry sparse fields and potentially sensitive attachments. Auto-close can permanently dismiss customer requests without resolution confirmation.

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: Recommended subagents, prompt patterns, and failure modes for agent-driven GitLab board operations.

list.item: board-author (sonnet) — synthesize board lists and scoped labels from a process YAML description.
triage-agent (sonnet) — classify and route incoming issues by type, priority, and component; never auto-close.
mr-issue-linker (haiku) — enforce "Closes #N" discipline and matching scoped labels before merge.
cycle-time-watcher (haiku, scheduled) — pull lead/cycle time from issue events, alert on drift.
webhook-router (haiku) — receive GitLab events and route to the appropriate downstream agent.
quick-action-generator (haiku) — emit `/label /milestone /weight /estimate` snippets for templates.

example.title: Board-author prompt pattern
example.code: You are board-author. Inputs: process.yaml (stages with WIP limits, filters, label scheme). Emit (a) scoped labels to create with "::" syntax, (b) an array of board lists with order + WIP limit, (c) a smoke-test plan that creates a test issue and walks it through every transition. Return STRICT JSON. Refuse if any stage lacks an exit criterion.

example.title: Triage-agent prompt pattern
example.code: You are triage-agent. Given an incoming issue (title, body, project context, existing labels), output: { "type": "bug|feature|tech-debt|docs|question", "priority": "critical|high|medium|low", "component": "<from project list>", "workflow": "ready|backlog", "reasons": [...] } Cite spans from the body that support each tag. Do not invent components not in the project's list.

antipattern.description: Using Personal Access Tokens with wide scopes for bot operations.
antipattern.reason: Prefer Group or Project Access Tokens scoped to "api" + "write_repository" only. Wide-scope PATs create blast radius on compromise.

antipattern.description: Bot-created issues notifying all project subscribers.
antipattern.reason: Bot users create issues as bot-owned; downstream notifications fan out to thousands. Use a dedicated bot user with notifications muted.

antipattern.description: Assuming free-tier feature parity for iteration cadences.
antipattern.reason: Iteration cadence creation requires Premium. Agents written against Premium features break on free-tier customer projects.

rule.statement: Apply human-in-the-loop checkpoints for: board structure changes, label-scheme changes, auto-close rules, and group-level moves.
rule.rationale: These operations affect all team members simultaneously and are difficult to reverse cleanly.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Command-line tools for GitLab board automation:

list.item: `glab` — First-party GitLab CLI: issues, MRs, labels, boards. Install: https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/cli
`glab-cli/glab issue` — Issue CRUD with quick-action support.
`python-gitlab` — Powerful Python client. Install: `pip install python-gitlab`
`node-gitlab` — Node client. Install: `npm i @gitbeaker/node`
`git push -o ci.skip` / `merge_request.create` — Push-options drive MR creation with labels. Docs: https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/push_options.html
`pre-commit` + `gitlab-ci-lint` — Validate `.gitlab-ci.yml` and label discipline locally.
`webhook` (adnanh) — Local webhook receiver for development. Install: https://github.com/adnanh/webhook
`mermaid-cli` — Render board state as a Mermaid flow. Install: `npm i -g @mermaid-js/mermaid-cli`

section.name: services-integrations
section.p: Services and integrations for GitLab boards:

list.item: GitLab.com SaaS (REST + GraphQL) — Easiest to integrate; SaaS rate limits apply.
GitLab Self-Managed Omnibus (REST + GraphQL) — Same APIs; admin-configured rate limits.
GitLab Dedicated (REST + GraphQL) — Single-tenant Cloud; same APIs.
GitLab Service Desk (REST) — Email-to-issue; agents triage; pair with `triage-agent`.
GitLab Runner Docker / k8s — Run automation as scheduled CI jobs.
Mattermost (REST) — First-class GitLab integration; pair for chatops.
Slack / Teams (REST) — Webhook routing for issue events.
Jira/Linear cross-link (REST) — Bridge integrations; mark source-of-truth.
Confluence / Notion / Wiki.js (REST) — Process and label-scheme docs.
LinearB / Swarmia / Sleuth (REST) — Engineering metrics overlay if native is insufficient.

template.purpose: Script to seed standard scoped labels (`workflow::*`, `priority::*`, `type::*`) via REST API.
GitLab issue template for bug reports with quick-action footer.
GitLab issue template for feature requests with AC checklist.

script.purpose: Create the standard scoped-label set on a GitLab project.
